rulings.Case Title: LEUA KHAMVAN VS. PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Extinguish Lien Rights Of Employer Addams Tree Service And Washington State Depot Of Labor & Industry
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, DECEMBER 17, 2007, LINE 4. PLAINTIFF LEUA KHAMVAN'SMotion To Extinguish Lien Rights Of Employer Addams Tree Service And Washington State Depot Of Labor & Industry: The motion is granted. (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR COMPLETE TENTATIVE RULING) Applying the governmental interest choice of law test, the Court concludes that the law of California controls. There is a conflict of law with respect to the rights of the party (Washington State Department of Labor & Industries) which paid worker compensation benefits under the facts of this case. The conflict arises as a consequence of the right of third parties in California to charge the employer with negligence, a right that does not exist in the State of Washington. The lien claimant contends that Washington has a greater interest because the employer and Mr. Khamvan were residents of Washington and Mr. Khamvan was compensated for his injury under the Washington's industrial insurance act. At the same time, it is undisputed that the accident occurred in California, the other alleged wrongdoers were all doing business in California, the litigation proceeded under California's comparative fault system and the funds at issue were recovered based on California's comparative fault system. Central to that system is the ability to evaluate the conduct of the employer. This has an impact on the ability of worker compensation insurers to recover payments made to the employer. To protect insurers, they are given the right to intervene and protect their interests. The lien holder was given that opportunity in this case and declined to exercise that right. The fact that the lien holder is not an insurance carrier but an agency of the State of Washington does not alter its rights as a lien holder. When addressing what plaintiff is entitled to recover, it is in the interest of California for its laws to be applied in a consistent manner to determine what plaintiff is entitled to recover and to determ

rulings.Case Title: LOBDELL CAPITAL PARTNERS et al VS. LEHMAN BROTHERS INC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Deft'S 1) Petition To Compel Arbitration And 2) Motion To Stay Proceedings; Compendium Of Non-California Authorities
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, DECEMBER 17, 2007, LINE 9. DEFENDANT LEHMAN BROTHERS, INC.'S DEFENDANTS' 1) Petition To Compel Arbitration And 2) Motion To Stay Proceedings: The motion is granted. Although P's claim centers on D's recommendation to become a limited partner of Tenet, P's investment in Tenet is governed by the agreements that P signed. In those agreements, D is described as the prime broker, the general partner is authorized to act on behalf of the partnership and each limited partner appoints the general partner to sign agreements on behalf of the limited partners. One of those agreements is to arbitrate a broad range of disputes with the broker D. P's claim centers on the loss of money due to the investment in Tenet as a consequence of investments that Tenet made after P became a limited partner. The contention that the arbitration agreement is not enforceable because D terminated the relationship with Tenet is not supported by admissible evidence. Assuming it is, the record is undisputed that at the time P invested not only was there a Tenet account at D, but P's funds were placed in that account and were governed by the documents P executed when P became a limited partner. =(302/PJM/VC)

rulings.Case Title: KGO TELEVISION INC VS. MUNICIPAL TRANSPORTATION AGENCY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On First Amended Verified Petition For Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, DECEMBER 17, 2007, LINE 12. PETITIONER KGO TELEVISION, INC.'S Hearing On First Amended Verified Petition For Writ Of Mandate: (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR COMPLETE TENTATIVE RULING) Respondents reading of the Court's Order of April 12, 2007 is reasonable for the Order precludes the City from disclosing personally identifying information as to 25 operators. Since that Order, Petitioner has utilized the provisions of the Sunshine Ordinance, the Public Records Act and the information it obtained pursuant to the April 12, Order to compile specific information related to the identification of certain drivers. It appears from the record that the source of Petitioner's current information consists of public records, specifically files related to tort claims. It is undisputed that the records being sought - the videos - are public records and must be disclosed absent a statutory exception. Respondents bear the burden of demonstrating that an exception applies and the exception at issue is one for "personnel, medical or similar files." Govt. Code § 6255. The evidence does not support the conclusion that the exception applies. The video cameras are on the bus, first and foremost, for public protection and record events that unquestionably are occurring in public. There has been no showing that the videos are part of a personnel or medical file or that the videos are similar to a personnel or medical file The privacy rights of drivers have been asserted as a further ground to deny the request. There simply is no legal support for the proposition that a person driving a bus has some inherent right of privacy. A driver's uniform has a number on it to facilitate a passenger's ability to identify the driver; the vehicles are on public streets performing a public function; and passengers are apprised as they enter the bus that their actions are being recorded. Plainly, the drivers also are on notice. Any uncertainty on this question is d
